paragraphs.text: I said that one of the problems we must face and resolve is this: Where should I direct my prayer? The answer I have it is suggested that we should direct it towards ourselves. While the prayer that you try to offer to God may not be important and significant to you first, you should not present it to the Lord. If not Are you attentive to the words you speak, if your heart does not? respond to them or if their life is not going in the same direction as theirs prayer will not be directed towards God. So the first thing, as I have to choose a sentence that matches all of your thoughts, all your heart and all your will, a prayer that does not have to be necessarily a model of liturgical art, but it must be sincere, something that does not fall short of what you want to say. You must understand this sentence with all the richness and precision that pose 23

In the use of words, there are three things we can do. Use spontaneous sentences, the type of sentence that springs from our souls; we can use small vocal sentences that are short, extremely dense and broad in content, so that have as many meanings as possible; and we can use what is sometimes calls, in a rather unpleasant sense, pret-aporter", ranging from the most bland produced by people trying to invent sentences for all occasions, to the most expressions deep of the saints, expressed in prayers that were not invented, but what the Holy Spirit imprinted in their lives and within their hearts. I would like to say something about each of those categories.

Spontaneous prayer is possible in two situations: in moments of that we have been vividly aware of the presence of God, when that awareness asks us for a response of worship, of joy, all the forms of response that we are capable of giving, with sincerity and in the face of the living God, or when we realize suddenly from the mortal danger we are in when we head towards to God, moments when we suddenly shout at him from the depth of despair and abandonment, and also from the feeling that there is no hope of salvation for us, less than God save us.

These two situations are the two extreme poles: the vision of ourselves. same in a desperate situation we are in, without God, alone, longing and yet unable to move; or the wonder of suddenly find ourselves face to face with God, when we can pray spontaneously, and it doesn't matter what words we use. We can repeat "my joy, my joy". We can say words, because they words don't matter, words are merely a support for a feeling, de hablar sin sentido, alocadamente, de nuestro amor y nuestra despair. Do you remember the passage from the Gospel regarding the Transfiguration, in which Peter says to Christ: "We will make three tents, for you, for Moses and for Elijah." The Gospel says that he was saying nonsense because he was out of his mind. He was feeling before something so overwhelming, that it said the first thing that came to mind head; said the first nonsense that expressed her feelings.
Now, if we imagine that we can hold sentences spontaneous throughout life, we make a childish mistake. The 24

spontaneous prayer must spring from our souls, we cannot do just turn a button and take it out. It is not at our mercy obtain it at any time. It comes from the depths of our soul, of something wonderful or unfortunate, but it does not occur in any situation in which we are neither excited about the divine presence, nor excited by the feeling of what we are and the position in which we find ourselves. So, at those moments, trying using spontaneous speech is an absolutely illusory exercise.

There are whole seasons in which we are neither at the bottom of the sea nor at the summit of the mountain, and that is the season when you cannot to pray spontaneously, but out of conviction. This is very important, because many people who start a life of prayer think that if he does not feel deeply the words and phrases he uses, he is not sincere. This is not true. One can sometimes be perfectly sincere in the clarity of his mind, in the righteousness of his will, although at a given moment those words or gestures do not express what one feels then.

The example that comes to my mind is this. When you live with su familia y usted trabaja fuera y se dedica a un trabajo duro puede getting home physically broken. If at that moment his mother, his Sister, your father or whoever it may be asks you: "Do you love me?" You will say: "Yes"; If the person continues asking: "Do you really me What do you want at this moment?” What you would honestly say is: “No, I feel nothing but the pain in my back and the exhaustion of it all.

"my body." But are you sincere when you say: "I love you," because You know that beneath all exhaustion there is a living current. of love. And when Christ says: 'Those who love me will keep' my commandments," does not say that "if you love me you will pass from a emotion to another, from one state of ecstasy to another, from a vision theological to another." It says no more: "If you believe in my word, live according to her", and in that "lives" means to overdo it a little own means. Do more than you could do spontaneously.

So there is a need to create a type of prayer that is not spontaneous, but truly rooted in the conviction. To find it you can borrow material from the great number of existing sentences. We already have a rich assortment of sentences that have been obtained in agonies of faith, by the Spirit Saint. For example, we have the psalms, we have many prayers. long and short in the liturgy of all Churches, that we can 25

take advantage. What matters is that you learn and know enough of such sentences so that at the right moment I can find the correct sentence. It's a matter of learning by heart quite a few significant passages, from the psalms or from the prayers of the saints. Each of us is more sensitive to certain passages. particulars. Mark those passages that reach your heart, that deeply moves them, that have a meaning, that express something from their own experience, whether of sin either of divine happiness or of struggle. Learn those passages, because one day, when he is so completely demoralized, so deeply desperate that I cannot extract any expression from his soul spontaneous, no word, will discover that those words arise and they are offered as a gift from God, as a gift from the Church, as a gift of holiness, helping our simple lack of energy. And then you really need the sentences that you have learned and made their own.

In the Orthodox Church, we have morning and evening prayers that they are generally longer than the ones usually used in West. It would require, approximately, half an hour in the morning and half an hour in the afternoon to read those sentences. It will be about learn them by heart, so that at other times they can to resort to those texts. But it's not enough to just learn sentences. from memory. If they are not 'lived', if life and prayer are not interwoven, the sentences become a courteous madrigal that Offer to God in the moments that are dedicated to Him.

If in your morning prayers you have said a phrase, you must behave according to that phrase throughout the day. And I believe that, apart from learning so many pages full of meaning as you he/she may, must establish the rule that when he/she has discovered a a phrase that makes sense to you - in the reading of the New or of the Old Testament, in general, or in the prayer with words of the liturgy -- it must try to apply it in the course of the day inexorably, as long as possible. You can imagine that it is able to take a phrase like that and live according to it throughout an entire day.

But it is extraordinarily difficult. If you can stay in a a sentence spoken for an hour without breaking the rule, will be You are lucky; but do it! Say: 'I have read this prayer, my my heart is willing, oh Lord, my heart is willing, during I propose that my heart be open to God for half an hour and 26

ready to obey his will." Just half an hour, then take a breath and think of something else, because if you try to to stay in line with a demanding and difficult phrase, in the end you will simply tell yourself: 'I can't do it anymore' and will end up doing nothing. But if you say: 'I have three or four o cinco frases como "slogans" para el día de hoy y trataré de aplicar since the moment I read it until ten o'clock tomorrow, then I will move on to the next one, then to the next one," you will see that gradually all the words of the sentence, all the thoughts and the feelings that the saints express in their prayers come to life for you, they begin to deeply penetrate your will and to shape his will and his body, because it is to his body that he owes apply the commandments.

However, you can say: 'I don't feel strong enough.' regarding these words. If those words express a conviction basic, but you don't feel anything at a certain moment, Turn to God with repentance and tell Him: 'This is my faith "Christian fundamentalist and, look, I don't feel anything," and then, from there, you may discover that suddenly a sentence springs to your mind spontaneous. You can express to God your sorrow, your misery, your displeasure against himself, and to insist on his desire to tell God the truth and that his aspiration is to do his will.

One last way we can pray is through the use, more or less continuing, from a vocal phrase that serves as a background, like a staff to throughout the day and throughout life. What I have in mind now it is something that is specifically used in the Orthodox Church. It is what we call the 'Jesus prayer', a prayer that is centered on the name of Jesus. "Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, sinner." This prayer is used by monks and nuns and also our laypeople. It is the prayer of stability, because it is the prayer it is not rhetoric -- we are not jumping from one thought to another --, it is a prayer that brings us face to face with God through the profession of faith concerning Him and defines the situation concerning we. It is the profession of faith that, according to the thought of the majority of orthodox ascetics and mystics, summarize the Gospels.

We profess that Jesus is our Lord, His sovereign right over we, the fact that He is our Lord and our God, and that it implies that our whole life is in his hands and that we submit to his will and not to anything else. This is the name of 'Jesus', in the 27

we confess the reality of the Incarnation and everything that it Incarnation represents Christ, in whom we see the Incarnate Word. of God in the line of the Old and New Testament, the anointed of Yahweh. Then, the perfect profession of faith, of who He is —the son of Dios—. Esto no es solamente una profesión de fe en Jesucristo, sino that also opens a path to the Trinity, because He is the Son of Father and no one can recognize in the prophet of Galilee the Son.

Incarnate of God, unless the Holy Spirit teaches him to see, understand and commit himself. So here we have the fourth profession of faith that allows us to be face to face with God in truth and profess in spirit. And then, 'have mercy' is the translation of "Eleison". When you say "Kyrie Eleison" you are using the Greek words that mean 'Lord, have mercy.' Quiero insistir en esas palabras que decimos en oración, porque en in all modern languages, words have become specialized and reducido en significado, en contraste con las lenguas antiguas. A We use extremely rich words to pray, but we do not realize the depth of what we say, because we take words according to what they mean in usage current, while they could have a deep echo in our hearts if we relate them to other things that we know.

I would like to give an example of this that would amaze scholars. of the classical languages, because the involved philology is questionable, but since it is based on a play on words made centuries ago by the Greek spiritual parents who knew their language and did not They were ashamed to make a pun, I will take advantage of it. me too. Many of us use the words 'Kyrie Eleison' O 'Lord, have mercy' at certain moments in our lives. We are aware at least that they exist and we know approximately what we want to say with them. In lines Generally, it is a plea to God for mercy, compassion, and cordiality. affection. Now, the student of classical languages would find an error in my explanation and in the Greek spiritual Fathers, because some from them derives 'eleison' from the same root as the Greek words "olive", "olive", "olive oil". However, let's leave the discussion to the scholars and let's take a look at what it can mean for we from the perspective of the Scriptures. When we say "Lord, have mercy", we can understand it simply as a plea. general to the mercy of God. In this case, it will not satisfy us, 28

why we can't put our entire life into this 'God have' mercy," and besides, the same words do not mean much in the common tongue. But if you think of the olive, of the olive of the Old and of the New Testament, you will see the following: the first time that the olive and the olive branch appears at the end of the Flood, when the branch is brought to Noah by the dove. (Is it the same dove that hovered over Christ on the day of his baptism?) This olive branch means that the the wrath of God has come to an end, that forgiveness is given freely, that A new time and new possibilities open up before us. This It is the first consideration. However, we cannot always go on this path, because it is not enough to have time and new possibilities opened before us if we are sick in our heart, if we lack of will or if we are mentally or physically unable to to discern or to follow that path. We need healing, so remember the oil that the Good Samaritan poured on the wounds of man who had fallen victim to the thieves. The healing power of the will of God makes it possible for us to take advantage of the cease from your anger, from the gift of forgiveness that is offered and, also, from the gift of time and space and eternity.

Another image is that of the anointing of priests and kings, which among the people of Israel were called to be on the threshold, among the divine world and the human world, between unity and harmony of the will of God and diversity and complexity -- not to mention the tensions and resistances -- of the human world. In order to to stay there, a man needs more than human capacity; it requires a divine gift. That was indicated by the anointing of priests and of kings. But in the New Testament all of us we are priests and kings and our vocation as human beings and Christians are beyond what a human being can reach. They calls us to be, and to effectively be, living members of the Body of Christ, temples of a pure land worthy of the Holy Spirit and co-participants of the divine nature. All of this is beyond our human capacity, and yet, we need to be human in full, in the deep way that a Christian thinks about the humanity in the image of the Incarnate Son of God. To do this we need the grace and help of God. All of this is shown to us in the same image.
Now, if we reflect with the same simplicity -- we only have to it requires a dictionary and a Bible and to think a little -- and yes 29

we think so simply and directly about the other words that we use in the sentence, then we will be presented with richer ones intellectually speaking. Then we could pay more attention to what we say, and our prayer would not simply be a collection of empty words or words that are mere symbols of something that has lost its true meaning. So, before to say 'Kyrie eleison', 'Lord, have mercy on me; Lord, show me your compassion; Lord, pour out your love and your tenderness upon me," we would have thought about the state we are in.

Have we descended to the lowest point? Are we facing infinite possibilities, and at the same time, unable to give us Why are we deeply hurt? healed and, however, in the face of such a great calling that us Is it humble to think of her because she is superior to us? Without embargo, it can only be fulfilled if God gives us the power to do so.

This implies a careful search for words. It also implies such treatment of words, which participate in our emotions and for us to take them and gather around them all the intensity and depth of our personal life. But if the words we use do not become reality because of our way of life, they will have no meaning and will lead nowhere, because they will be like a bow that we cannot tension due to lack of string. It does not have absolutely object to asking God for something that we are not willing to do. If we say: 'Oh God, free me from this or that temptation," while at the same time we seek any possible means to fall into such temptation, hoping that now that God is in the control, He will take us out of it, then we will not have many possibilities of luck; God gives us strength, but we must use it.
When in our prayers we ask God to give us strength to do something in his name, we do not ask him to do it in ours place, because we are too weak to want to do it ourselves same.

The lives of the saints are enlightening in this regard and are described an occasion like this in the life of Saint Philip Neri. He was a man irritable, who argued easily and had violent outbursts of cholera, and of course endured violent fits of intolerance from his siblings. One day he thought that this could not continue. If it was because virtue or if he could not hold on against his brothers, his 'Vita' does not tell us.
The fact is that she escaped to the chapel, fell at the feet of Christ and gave him he asked to be freed from his anger. Then he left filled with hope. 30

the first person he met was one of his brothers, who I had never provoked the slightest anger in him, but for the first time in his. that brother was offensive and unpleasant to him. So Felipe let himself be carried away by anger and went to look for another of his brothers, who had always been like his cloth of tears and he he had consoled. But even this man replied to him brusquely.

So Felipe returned to the chapel, fell at the feet of the statue of Christ said: 'Oh, Lord, did I not ask you to deliver me from my anger?' And the Lord answered him: 'Yes, Philip, and for this reason I am multiplying the chances that you learn.

I think it is very important for us to realize that God he will act that way. He is not going to be crucified by you every day. There is a moment when you must take up your own cross. Each of us must to take up our own cross, and when we ask for something in our prayers, we must assume that we will do it with all our might, all the intelligence and all the enthusiasm we can put into our actions and with all the value and energy that we have. In addition, we do this with all the power that God gives us. If we do not do it this way, We are wasting time praying. This implies that 'Kyrie eleison' or similar words that we can pronounce turn against us. Our mind must be formed, shaped by those words, filled and in harmony with them. Our heart must accept them with complete conviction and express them with all the strength of what we are capable, and our will must take them and transform them into action. Therefore, prayer and action should be two expressions of the same situation vis-à-vis God, of us and of everything around us. If we don't do it this way, we waste time. What about What is worth telling God things when He gives us the strength to fight them sitting and waiting for Him to do it for us? What interest has in repeating words that have become so fine, so without a sense that allows us to weave a web among us and God?

Therefore, choose the right words, select them and ignite all the attention to them, because they are words of truth, and the words that God will listen, because they are true. Put all your heart into it. make those words so alive with awareness intellectual, because they are true, and make them transcend your interior to the depths of your own heart. 31

The words of the sentence have the quality of always being words. of commitment. You cannot simply say words of sentence without implying: 'If I say this, I mean what I am going to do' when the occasion arises." When you say to God: "At all costs, at all costs, oh Lord, save me", you must remember that you must put put all your will into it, because one day God will tell you: 'This is the price what has to be paid." The authors of antiquity used to say: "Give your blood and God will give you the spirit." That is the price: Give up everything, you will receive heaven; abandon slavery, you will acquire freedom. Like you will is committed not only in the act of praying, but in everything the consequences of prayer, your body must also be involved, because the human being is not simply an integrated soul temporarily in a body. It is a being composed of body and soul, a unique being that is man.

One must make a physical effort in prayer, the physical attention, the physical way of praying. Fasting, if food weighs too much on you To pray, they also have to consider this. If you do this, you will be knocking on the door.

So, if we want to go inward with all those words, to delve more and more deeply, in the way it is done to extract something from the bowels of the earth, we must take risks, and this risk is that it is very difficult to enter into ourselves. It seems simple. We all assume that we are already within ourselves, and the deeper we reach, the more enjoyment it will bring us. No It's as simple as that. It's true that when we have reached a certain depth, everything is fine, but along the way it resembles a lot to the stories about the quest for the Holy Grail. There are all kinds of monsters that assault us on the road, and the monsters are not Demons, they are not our neighbors, we are ourselves. This it becomes more unpleasant and much more difficult.

In general, it is greed, fear, and curiosity that make us to live outward. A French scientist who worked in America, Alexis Carrel said in a book titled 'The Unknown of Man' that if you ask yourself where your personality ends, you will see that the tongue of a greedy person extends like tentacles towards all the edible things in the world; the eyes of a curious person they are like tentacles projected and grasped onto everything that exists around; the ears of the one who listens in secret become long and 32

they are wide and reach long distances. If you can make a drawing of yourself in in these terms, you will see how little of you remains inside, because everything he is extroverted. So the first thing one should do is release the tentacles and collect them. You cannot delve into yourself if you are totally turned outward.

Try an experiment and you will see, you will discover a large number of others. useful things on the go. Try to find time to be alone with yourself same: close the door and make yourself comfortable in your room in a a moment when I have nothing to do. Say: "Now I am with myself," and sit with oneself. After a very short time time lapse will surely feel boring. This teaches us a very useful thing. This gives us the opportunity to perceive the fact that if after ten minutes of being alone with ourselves we we feel this way, it's no surprise that others feel similarly Bored! Why? It's like this because we have little to offer each other. ourselves as subjects of thought, of emotion and of life. If you look at your life carefully, you will soon discover we rarely live from the inside out; on the contrary, we respond to incitements, to excitations from the outside. In other words, we live from reflections, from reactions. Something happens and we respond, someone speaks and we answer. But when we are left without anything that stimulates us? to think, talk or act we give ourselves tells that there is very little inside us that drives us to the action, in any direction. This is certainly a discovery dramatic. We are completely empty, we do not act from our interior, but we accept as our life a life that it is actually fed from outside; we get used to that things happen that push us to do other things. How few sometimes we can live simply from the depth and the richness that we suppose to have in ourselves!

There is a passage in the 'Pickwick Papers' by Charles Dickens that it's a very good description of my life, and probably of yours too of his life. Pickwick goes to the club. He hires a horse-drawn carriage and in the The journey asks many questions. Among the questions, it says: 'Tell me How is it possible that such a weak and miserable horse can pull this? large and heavy carriage?" The coachman replies: "It is not a matter of horse, sir; it's a matter of the wheels," and Mr. Pickwick says: "What Do you mean? The coachman says, "Look, we have a magnificent pair of wheels that are so well greased, that it suffices for the horse to pull 33

a little for the wheels to turn, and then the horse can run at their whim." This is the way we live most of of time. We are not the horse that pulls, we are the horse that runs escaping from the carriage out of fear for life.

As we do not yet know how to act without an external reason, we discovered that we do not know what to do with ourselves, and then we started to feel increasingly bored. So it First of all, you must learn to sit in your own company. confront boredom and draw all conclusions possible.

After a while, the situation is worse than boredom, because it is not We are simply bored, like to say: 'I am a person' active and useful for my neighbor. I always do good, and for me being in Inactivity, without doing something for others, is a harsh test.

We started to discover something more. We got bored trying to get out of that situation looking inward to see if there is something with to put an end. Soon we realize that there is nothing, since that everything we have to think about we have already thought dozens of times times. The entire range of emotions that we store is there, like a plan that we have closed because we are not used to that the piano plays alone. We need to have someone to play the keys. We are not accustomed to being idle, that is why it makes us uneasy and it can lead us to the point of causing us distress. If you read the Fathers of the Desert, who had a lot of experience with this, or to the monks who spent their lives in monasteries, you will see that there are moments when they ran out of their cells shouting for help, trying to find someone, something, no matter what. The demon it would have been better than this solitude of self-contemplation.
One of the spiritual writers, Theophan the recluse, says: 'Much People are like a curled wood shavings around their interior.
"empty." If we are truly honest, we must admit that this is a very appropriate definition practically of the state of everyone us.

Then we have to be prepared to fight against this anguish. and say: 'No, I will hold on and I will reach as far as the same' anguish propelled me to do what goodwill cannot. In Truth, there comes a moment of desperation, anguish, and terror that even makes us turn more inward and shout: "Lord, 34

Have mercy. I perish, Lord, save me!" We discovered that there is no Nothing that can give life or that is life; that everything we call life, we imagine being life, it was outside and that inside there is nothing.
Then we peered into the abyss of nullity and found that the deeper we delve, the less we find something of ours. It is a dangerous moment, it is the moment when we must doubt.

At this point we have reached the first level of depth where we are starting to be ready to knock on a door. Because in the level where we rest from our neighbor, before feeling ourselves bored, at the level where we are simply bored and offended for being there, at the level where we begin to feel worry and discomfort and then a little anxiety, we do not have yet reason to cry and scream with a desperation that is overwhelming totally our mind, our heart, our will, our body, with the feeling that, unless God comes, I am lost, there is no hope, because I know that if I surface, from that depth, I will meet again in the kingdom of the disillusionment, of life as a reflection, but not of real life.

This is the point from which we can start to call a door. that is still closed, but beyond which there is hope, that hope that Bartimaeus, the blind man at the gates of Jericho, felt from the depths of her despair, when Christ passed by of him.

We know from the Gospels that Bartimaeus was by the side of the path, blind without remedy, without faith or hope in human help and reduced to begging to make a living, not really expecting in the charity (the word means care), but in the kind of charity that it consists of throwing some coins at someone you don't even know looked. And one day this man, who has already given up hope, who was sitting on the dust in his total blindness, heard talk of the man, a new prophet who was performing miracles on Earth Santa. If he had had eyes he probably would have run away. through the country until finding him, but I couldn't follow the movements of that maker of wonders. So he remained where he was and the the existence of one, who could possibly heal him, must have increased his desperation, to sharpen it. And one day he perceived the passage of a 35
multitude, a multitude that resembled no other multitudes.

Probably, like all blind people, he had developed the sense a hearing and a finer sensitivity than ours, because he asked: 'Who is passing by here?' And they said to him: 'Jesus of Nazareth'. And then he stood up in a fit of extreme desperation and of extreme hope. Extreme hope, because Christ was at his scope, but in the background, latent, the despair because in a few a few steps could be next to Bartimaeus and a few steps they could separate him from it and he probably would not cross paths again his path never again. And driven by that desperate hope he began to shout and cry out: 'Jesus, son of David, have mercy on me.' It was a perfect profession of faith. And at that moment, as his desperation was so great, she was able to draw from herself such bold hope. to be healed, saved, recovered. And Christ heard him.

There is a degree of desperation that is fused to the total and perfect. hope. This is the point at which, by having introduced ourselves into we ourselves will be ready to pray; and then "Lord, have mercy" is enough. We don't need to do any. of the elaborate speeches found in manuals of It's quite something to simply exclaim in the middle of the desperation: "Help!" and you will be heard.

We often do not find enough intensity in our prayer, enough conviction, enough faith, because our despair does not it is quite deep. We want God, in addition to many others things that we have, we want your help, but simultaneously we tried to find support anywhere, and we kept God in the reserve like our last cartridge. We turn to the princes and to the sons of men and we say: 'Oh God, give them the strength to that they help me." We rarely set aside the princes and the sons of men and we say: 'I am not going to ask for help from anyone, I prefer Your help. If our desperation comes from enough depth, if what we ask for and cry out for is so essential that contains all the needs of our life, then we will find words of prayer and we will be able to reach the center of prayer and meet with God.

And now, something more about the confusion surrounding us. The topic it Bartimaeus also cries out. He shouted, but what does the Gospel say? what were those around doing? They were trying to silence him, and 36

we can see the pious people who saw correctly, they had a good legs and good health, around Christ, talking about elevated themes, the Kingdom that is to come, the mysteries of the Holy Scriptures, turning to Bartimaeus and saying to him: 'Well, Can't you be quiet? Your eyesight, your eyesight, what does that matter when Do you talk to God? Bartimaeus was like someone who stands out of order. to ask God for something that one desperately needs while some ceremony is being celebrated, and it breaks the harmony of the acts. You will be immediately thrown out of the place. You will be silenced. But The Gospel also says that, despite all those people who loved him to silence, he insisted because it was something that mattered to him the more they imposed silence on him, the more he shouted.

This is my message. There is a saint in Greece named Maximus, a a young man who once went to church and listened to the reading of the Epistle, he said that we must pray incessantly. It impressed him so much way he thought he could do nothing more than fulfill that commandment. He left the temple, went up to some nearby mountains and he began to pray incessantly. As a Greek peasant of the fourth century, he knew the Our Father and some other prayers. So he started to recite them, as he told us, again and again. Then he began to feeling very happy. I was praying, I was with God, I felt joyful, Everything seemed perfect, except that the sun began to set and it became At night it began to get cold, and as it darkened, I started to hear everything. class of unsettling rumors: branches that were breaking under the weight of the beasts, electrifying eyes, the moan of the little beasts attacked by the strongest beasts, and so on. Then he felt truly alone, like a small creature without protection in a world of dangers, of death, of murders and that he had no help unless God gave it to him. He did not recite it again.

Our Father and the Creed; he did exactly what Bartimaeus, he began to shout: 'Lord Jesus Christ, son of God, have mercy on me.' And he screamed like this all night because the animals and the electrifying eyes did not they gave him the opportunity to sleep. Then morning arrived and he thought that As all the beasts had gone to sleep: "Now I can pray." but by then he was hungry. He thought he would pick some blackberries and headed towards a thicket, but realized that the eyes electrifying and the wild claws could be hidden among the weeds. So she started to walk very slowly and with each step she said: "Lord Jesus Christ, save me, save me, save me. Oh God, help me," "protect me"; and for every blackberry I ate, I had prayed several times. 37

Time passed and after many years he found a elderly and experienced ascetic, who asked him how he had learned to pray incessantly. Máximo said: "I believe it was the demon who taught me to pray non-stop." The other man said: I think I understand what you mean, but I would like to be sure. that I understood you correctly." Máximo explained how he I had been gradually getting used to all the noises and dangers. of the day and of the night. But then the temptations came, temptations of the flesh, temptations of the mind, of the emotions, and later violent attacks of the demon. After that, there was no moment of the day or night that would not turn towards God, saying: 'Have mercy, have mercy, help me, help me, help me." Then, after fourteen years like this, the Lord spoke to him. appeared, and from the moment God appeared, the tranquility, peace and serenity flooded over him. There was no more fear — of the darkness, from the underbrush, nor fear of the devil—; the Lord took him away I can. 'Back then,' said Máximo, 'I had learned that, to unless the Lord Himself comes, I have no hope or help. So that even when he was serene, at peace and happy, he continued praying: "Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me," because he knew that only in divine mercy there was peace of heart and peace of spirit and bodily tranquility and rectitude of will.

So Maximus learned to pray, not despite the prevailing confusion, but because of the confusion and because the confusion was a danger authentic. If we could realize that we are in a confusión mucho mayor, que el demonio está al acecho, tratando de cogernos y destruirnos, que cada reunión humana es un juicio, es crisis, it is a situation in which we are called to receive Christ or to be messengers of Christ for the person we meet, yes we understood that all life has this intensity of meaning, then we would be ready to shout and pray continuously and the confusion would not be an obstacle, but the precise condition that allows us we are taught to pray while our inexperience prevents us from praying from the deepest without help, without any encouragement to do so.

When we know nothing about prayer, when we have not prayed not at all in our entire life, or not enough, how can we pray in the living conditions we experience? I have experience of this in different circumstances: on the days when I was exercising 38
medicine, five years during the war, in my stage as priest, and others, and it can be done. It can be done if one is enough simple to do. It can be done in the following way.

Wake up in the morning and as your first act, give thanks to God for Hello, even if you don't feel especially happy about the day that it wait. "This day, which the Lord has made, let us receive it with joy and "We thank him." Once you have done this, give yourself time. to recognize the truth of what has been said and to truly feel it (perhaps at a level of deep conviction and not in a way that could (to call oneself optimistic). And then, get up, wash yourself, clean yourself, do what you that I have to do and return to God. Return to God again with two convictions. One, that it belongs to God and the other, that this day is God's own property and is completely new and recent. That has never existed before. To put it simply with a Russian expression, it is like a vast territory covered in snow. No one it has not stepped yet. It is virgin and pure before you. And now, what Is it coming? What comes next is that you ask God to bless this day, everything in it should be blessed and governed by Him.

After this, you will act seriously because many times one "Oh God, bless me," and after receiving the blessing, we act like the prodigal son: we take all our belongings and we We march to a foreign country to live a life of disorder.

This day is blessed by God and it belongs to God and now, let's enter it. Delve into it like the very messenger of God; to whoever wants to find, finds him on the path of God. He is you are there to be the presence of the Lord God, the presence of Christ, the presence of the Spirit; the presence of the Gospel: this is its function on this particular day. God has never said when you you find yourself in a situation in HIS own Name, He will be crucified and you will come out victorious. You must be prepared to, in the name of God, as the Son of God did, go through situations successive situations of humiliation, with humility, in truth and willing to be pursued and so on. Generally, what we expect When we fulfill God's commandments, we see some results. wonderful right away; we read things like this, sometimes, in the lives of the saints. When, for example, we get slapped, we put the other one. cheek, without thinking even remotely that they are going to hit us, but instead waiting for the other person to say: 'Oh, what humility!'; you have has been rewarded and the other person saves their soul. But it does not happen from 39

this way. You pay for the damages and many times the punishment is harsh. The What matters is that you are prepared for it. As for the day, if you accept that this day has been blessed by God, chosen by God with his own hand, then all the people that he meets they are a gift from God, every circumstance I go through is a gift from God, whether it is bitter or sweet, whether you like it or dislike it. It is the very gift from God, and if he accepts it that way, he can face any situation. But you must face it knowing that you can pass anything, whether good or not, and if you walk in the name of God throughout a day that has come fresh and new from His own hands and that has been blessed so that you live in it, in that caso, puede usted complementar la oración y la vida como si fueran the two sides of a coin. You act and pray as one breathes, Because everything that happens requires the blessing of God.

Years ago, I talked about this in Taizé, and I maintained correspondence with about thirty boys and girls from there. One of them wrote to me. saying: 'I tried to follow your advice. I tried with all energy. I have not let a minute pass without acting and praying, acting and praying and now I cannot hear the word of God. I cannot bear this kind of sentence." I replied to him: "You've got an indigestion. You should to have used common sense in the sentence as it is used in life ordinary. You cannot start if you have never prayed before, with eighteen hours of dialogue and prayer with God continuously while you do other things. But you can easily book some minutes and concentrate on them with all your energy. Just come back the eyes towards God, smile at Him and move forward. There are moments when You can tell God: 'I simply need to rest a little.' I don't have the strength to be with you all the time," which is perfectly true. You are not yet capable of being in company Of God all the time. Well, go ahead, say it. God knows it.
Perfectly, do whatever you do. Step aside and say a moment: "I'm going to rest. For a moment, I accept being less perfect.

This way we can rest and contemplate the things that are also things of God —trees and buildings— and then, after a while, return to Yes. If we try to pray continuously, we will feel defeated very soon, but if we choose moments wisely, we will be able to do it. 40

If you do this, you will be able to pray. You can try it, but don't forget to be sober, because there is a sin that the Spiritual Fathers called "spiritual greed," which consists of wanting more and more from God, when you should go on a diet and be content with a a little, which is sufficient for their needs.
